EMPATHY: Something in her posture — she's been holding herself together for a long time.
There's a grief here that hasn't been spoken aloud yet.
You can feel it, the way a cold room feels — the absence of someone.
She doesn't want you to leave. She just doesn't know how to ask you to stay.
NOSE: The rice smells overcooked. She wasn't paying attention when she made it.
There's something under the cardamom and cumin — something sour. Guilt, maybe.
Old cigarette smoke on his collar. He told you he quit.
The tiffin came back half-eaten. That hasn't happened in weeks. Something changed.
You can smell the ghee from the stairwell. She cooked today. That's new.
STEADY_HANDS: Don't rush. A wobbling stack draws eyes you don't want.
You've navigated this alley a hundred times. The body knows even when the mind doesn't.
Hold it level. Some things only stay intact if you treat them gently.
This conversation needs the same care as a full tiffin on a crowded bus. Slow. Deliberate.
STREET_SENSE: The chai stall shutters at 6. If he's still standing there, he's waiting for something that isn't chai.
Two men watching from the shade. They're not regulars. File that away.
She took a different staircase. She didn't want to be seen going up.
The gully goes quiet this time of day. Good time to talk.
He's lying, but not about the big thing. About something smaller that protects the big thing.
GRIEF: You recognize that particular stillness. It's the stillness of someone who has stopped expecting things to get better.
There's a shape to this sadness — it isn't fresh. It's been worn smooth.
She counts her days in what she used to do with him. Today is a 'we used to go to the market on Tuesdays' kind of day.
He hasn't cried yet. That's the dangerous part.
The loss didn't happen all at once. It's still happening. Every day, a little more.
